g.data-narration: Welcome to Arithmetic Operators in Java - the building blocks of mathematical computations in programming.
Arithmetic operators enable mathematical computations - addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and modulus operations on variables.
g.text: They form the foundation for mathematical computations in Java programs.
Essential for algorithms, data manipulation, and OOP calculations.

g.data-narration: Java provides five binary arithmetic operators requiring two operands: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and modulus.
The addition operator (+) adds operands, also used for string concatenation. Subtraction (-) subtracts right from left operand.
Multiplication (*) has higher precedence than addition/subtraction. It operates on integers and floating-point numbers alike.
Integer division truncates decimals - 17/5 equals 3, not 3.4. Use float/double for precise decimal results.
The modulus operator (%) returns the remainder of division. Useful for checking divisibility and cyclic operations.
The increment (++) and decrement (--) operators add or subtract 1 from a value. They come in prefix and postfix forms.
Prefix (++x) increments first then returns new value. Postfix (x++) returns current value then increments - crucial for exams.
g.text: Prefix vs Postfix - CRITICAL FOR EXAMS
EXAM TIP: Prefix increments THEN uses, Postfix uses THEN increments

g.data-narration: Compound operators (+=, -=, *=, /=, %=) combine arithmetic with assignment and handle automatic type casting.
g.text: Shorthand: operation + assignment in one step

g.data-narration: Java follows operator precedence: postfix first, then unary, multiplicative (* / %), then additive (+ -).
Java automatically promotes smaller types: byte, short, char become int. Expression promotes to largest operand type.
g.text: Smaller types automatically promote to larger types:

g.data-narration: Let's see practical examples: integer division gives 3, modulus gives 2, floating-point division gives 3.333...
Avoid these exam pitfalls: forgetting integer division truncates, confusing prefix/postfix, and missing type promotion.
In summary: master binary operators (+, -, *, /, %), understand prefix/postfix increment, know type promotion rules for exams.
g.text: Integer division truncates • Prefix increments first • Use parentheses for clarity
